coredata.dc:title: Amino-modified carbon dots as a functional platform for drug delivery: Load-release mechanism and cytotoxicity
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Shi, Nana
Sun, Kaiyue
Zhang, Zedi
Zhao, Juan
Geng, Lina
Lei, Yuhua
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dot (CDs) were prepared for carrying DOX (Doxorubicin) or FAC (Ferric ammonium citrate), and the loading mechanism and release kinetics of CDs-drugs were studied. CDs were prepared using peony pollen and urea, and characterized by TEM, XRD, 13C NMR, XPS, UV, FT-IR, DLS and zeta potential. The obtained CDs were rich of –NH2 group on the surface, amorphous phase, spherical morphology with the diameter about 5 nm, and had a blue fluorescence with the quantum yield (QY) of 12%. Iron disorders can cause cancer or anemia. DOX is used for treating cancer, but has severe side effects. FAC is an iron supplement agent, but had low bioavailability. CDs carried DOX (or FAC) through –N=C– (or –CONH–) bond, had high loading efficiency for DOX (37.2%) and FAC (54.0%) by optimizing mass ratios of mCDs to mdrug and reacted overnight. Cellular experiments showed that CDs-drugs had lower cytotoxicity than free drugs and CDs co-located with the drug. In vitro release indicated that CDs-DOX had pH-targeted property and CDs-FAC could be absorbed easily. CDs-drug release belonged to Weibull model, and release mechanism was determined by the bonding way between CDs and drug. CDs had potential applications in delivery drugs with –C=O or –COOH group.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Iron metabolism
Oxidative stress
Drug release

We thank Dr. Yingqiang Zhao (Shandong Normal University) for help with the experimental measurements.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of CDs: (A) TEM, (B) Particle size distribution, (C) Zeta potential, (D) UV and PL spectra, (E) FT-IR spectra, (F) XRD pattern, (G) 13C NMR spectrum and (H) Color coordinate.
XPS of CDs: (A) Full spectrum, (B) C1s spectrum, (C) N1s spectrum, (D) O1s spectrum.
Characterization of CDs-DOX: (A) Optimization mass ratio of mCDs:mDOX, (B) ζ potential, (C) UV–vis spectra, (D) FL spectra.

Cell viability and photographs of the SH-SY5Y cells: (A) MTT of cells were incubation with CDs, free DOX or CDs-DOX for 24 h. (Data = mean ± SD; n = 6). **P < 0.01 compared to controls; ##P < 0.01 compared to DOX only. (B) Photographs under the microscope (200×) at a DOX-equivalent dose of 1.25 µg/mL in cells after 24 h treatment. (C) DAPI stained image with arrows indicating apoptotic nuclei.
Fluorescence co-localization of CDs and DOX in cells (40×). (CDs: e x = 405 nm, DOX: e x = 488 nm).

In vitro release of CDs-drugs: (A) CDs-DOX in PBS buffer (pH = 5.0, 6.5, and 7.4), (B) CDs-DOX in plasma, SIF and SGF at 37 °C; (C) in vitro release of CDs-FAC and (D) free FAC in plasma, SIF and SGF at 37 °C.

head.ce:title: Amino-modified carbon dots as a functional platform for drug delivery: Load-release mechanism and cytotoxicity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hebei Key Laboratory of Organic Functional Molecules, College of Chemistry and Material Science, Hebei Normal University, Shijiazhuang 050024, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hebei Key Laboratory of Organic Functional Molecules
College of Chemistry and Material Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hebei Key Laboratory of Organic Functional Molecules, College of Chemistry and Material Science, Hebei Normal University, Shijiazhuang 050024, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Basic Medicine, Hebei Medical University, Shijiazhuang 050017, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Basic Medicine, Hebei Medical University, Shijiazhuang 050017, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Functional carbon dot (CDs) were prepared for carrying drugs, and the load-release mechanisms and cytotoxicity of CDs-drugs were studied. The prepared CDs with –NH2 modifying can be used as carriers to transport drugs (containing –C=O group, such as DOX) to improve their pH targeting, or carry drugs (containing –COOH group, such as FAC) to improve their intestinal absorbability.

Carbon dot (CDs) were prepared for carrying DOX (Doxorubicin) or FAC (Ferric ammonium citrate), and the loading mechanism and release kinetics of CDs-drugs were studied. CDs were prepared using peony pollen and urea, and characterized by TEM, XRD, 13C NMR, XPS, UV, FT-IR, DLS and zeta potential. The obtained CDs were rich of –NH2 group on the surface, amorphous phase, spherical morphology with the diameter about 5 nm, and had a blue fluorescence with the quantum yield (QY) of 12%. Iron disorders can cause cancer or anemia. DOX is used for treating cancer, but has severe side effects. FAC is an iron supplement agent, but had low bioavailability. CDs carried DOX (or FAC) through –N=C– (or –CONH–) bond, had high loading efficiency for DOX (37.2%) and FAC (54.0%) by optimizing mass ratios of mCDs to mdrug and reacted overnight. Cellular experiments showed that CDs-drugs had lower cytotoxicity than free drugs and CDs co-located with the drug. In vitro release indicated that CDs-DOX had pH-targeted property and CDs-FAC could be absorbed easily. CDs-drug release belonged to Weibull model, and release mechanism was determined by the bonding way between CDs and drug. CDs had potential applications in delivery drugs with –C=O or –COOH group.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) with particle sizes less than 10 nm [1,2] have special optical properties and are non-toxic [3]. CDs have been successfully applied in bioimaging [4], catalysis [5], sensors [6–8], drug delivery [9,10], and so on. Especially, CDs has been developed greatly in drug loading. It is well known that untargeted delivery of drugs can cause toxicity to normal cells [9], but the good information is that CDs delivery system can improve this situation. Studies have shown that CDs can be used as nanocarriers of doxorubicin (DOX) for anticancer drug, and the CDs loading system have better anti-tumor effects than free DOX, efficiently inhibiting tumor growth and improving targeted therapy [9]. Jia group used PEG modified CDs to promote the spread of targeted drugs to the tumor microenvironment, which reduced the leakage of the drug in the physiological medium and limited the side effects of the drug, and therefore increased the antitumor activity of DOX [11].

Iron is one of the essential trace elements in the body and has important physiological functions in the body, such as oxygen transport, electron transfer, energy metabolism and DNA synthesis [12]. The disorder of iron metabolism can cause a variety of diseases [13]. Iron overload is known to induce reactive oxygen species (ROS), which can cause oxidative stress, DNA damage, apoptosis, neurodegenerative diseases, such as Parkinson’s disease (PD) and Alzheimer’s disease (AD), and even to cancer [9]. Cancer is a global disease now and DOX is a common drug for treating cancer in clinical [12]. Unfortunately, it can produce a wide range of side effects in the body, for example hair loss and heart damage [14]. More efforts need to be devoted to reduce its side effects and increase treatment efficiency. And our team did some work on anti-oxidative stress previously [15].

Iron deficiency is the most common nutritional disease in the world. It can lead to anemia and neurocognitive dysfunction of infants. Ferric ammonium citrate (FAC) is a common iron supplement, but it had low bioavailability. Besides, FAC is a photochemical sensitive material, easy deliquescence and hydrolysis in the air, and it has ammonia and iron metallic taste. Therefore, how to improve its stability and bioavailability is important. In our previous study, we used liposome as a carrier to enhance the physicochemical stability and bioavailability, achieving good effects [16,17].

In vitro release can contribute to establish in vitro–in vivo correlation [21]. In vitro release study reflected the combined effects of drug properties such as solubility and particle size and could serve as a surrogate of in vivo testing [20]. In vitro release tests could detect the changes of formulation or physicochemical characteristics of the formulation components that might correspond to the altered in vivo performance of the dosage form [22]. At present, there are few researches on the release model and mechanism of CDs-drug delivery system.

Carbon is the basic element of living things and CDs had no toxicity to animals [23]. Although CDs have been used in many fields, it has not been reported to be used as a drug carrier to treat iron disorder diseases in the iron metabolism field. In previous, we have reported that the hollow CDs loaded DOX system, and demonstrated that the groups on the HCD surface play an important role in loading [9]. We also reported that FAC encapsulated by liposome could increase serum iron levels higher than unencapsulated FAC and relieved anemia symptoms [16,17]. Based on these researches, in this work, CDs mainly with –NH2 groups were prepared using peony pollen as carbon resource and urea as modification reagent. The CDs were characterized by Uv–visible absorption (UV), transmission electron microscope (TEM), power X-ray diffraction (XRD), Carbon-13 nuclear magnetic resonance (13C NMR), zeta potential and Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), respectively. To prove CDs as a multifunctional nanocarrier, DOX and FAC were loaded respectively for their potential usage in treating iron metabolism diseases. The cell viability and drug release kinetics of CDs-DOX (or CDs-FAC) were investigated.

Peony pollen is obtained by drying and grinding peony flowers. KCl and NaCl were obtained from Yongda Science and Technology Co. LTD (Tianjin, China). CO(NH2)2, Na2HPO4 and KH2PO4 were purchased from Bodi Chemical Co. LTD (Tianjin, China). Quinine sulfate was obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. LTD (Shijiazhuang, China). Doxorubicin (DOX) and pepsin were the product of the shanghai yuanye bio-technology Co. LTD (Shanghai, China). Trypsin was purchased from Shanghai Lanji technology development Co. LTD (Shanghai, China). Plasma was purchased from Hebei blood center (Shijiazhuang, China). Ferric ammonium citrate (FAC) and EDC were obtained from Sigma–Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). The SH-SY5Y and HepG2 cells were purchased from the Cell Bank of Peking Union Medical College (Beijing, China).

Peony pollen (0.2 g) and urea (0.6 g) were dissolved in 10 mL distilled water, stirred, transferred to 30 mL Teflon autoclave, and then heated at 200 °C for 10 h. The reactor was naturally cooled to room temperature, and the black-brown solution was obtained. The solution was centrifuged for 10 min (4000 rpm/ min) to remove the precipitation. The supernatant was dialyzed through a dialysis bag (MWCO 1000 Da) in the 1 L distilled water to remove small molecular weight impurity. The dialysate was changed every 6 hours, and the obtained solution was kept at 4 °C for later use.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained using a JEM 100SX (Hitachi, Japan). UV–vis spectra were recorded on a U-3010 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) (cCDs = 0.0600 mg/mL). Fluorescence (FL) measurements were carried out on a F-4600 fluorospectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) (cCDs = 0.0040 mg/mL). Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a FTIR-8900 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) ranging from 500 to 4000 cm−1. The powder X-ray diffractionmeter (XRD) was performed on a D8 Advance X-ray Diffractometer (Bruker, Germany) with a Cu Ka radiation (40 kV, 40 mA) in the scanning speed of 0.03° s−1 at the range from 10° to 70°. The 13C NMR was recorded using an AVIII-500Q (Bruker, Germany). Zeta potential and hydrodynamic size were measured using a Nano-ZS90 (Malvern Instruments Ltd, Malvern, UK). XPS was taken on an ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray Photoelectron Spectrometer microprobe using a monochromatic Al-K X-ray source (h = 1486.68 eV) (Thermo Fisher, Waltham, MA, USA).

To calculate the quantum yield (QY) of CDs, low concentration solution of CDs was prepared (the absorption of UV–vis spectra was less than 0.05). The QY was obtained by a reference method (QY of quinine sulfate was equal to 0.55) [24].
(1)
Yu = Y s ·
Fu
Fs
·
As
Au

The following experiments were carried out under dark conditions: CDs and drug (DOX or FAC) were dissolved at different mass ratios (mCDs: mDOX = 1:0.5, 1:1, 1:1.5, 1:2, 1:2.5, 1:3, mCDs: mFAC = 1:0.5, 1:0.75, 1:1, 1:1.5, 1:2) and stirred overnight. The mixture (CDs-DOX or CDs-FAC) was dialyzed in redistilled water to remove the free drug (DOX or FAC), and fresh medium was changed every 6 h over a course of 12 h.
The DOX loading ratio was calculated by mDOX/mCDs × 100% [12].
Where mDOX and mCDs were the mass of DOX and CDs respectively, measured by the working curve of UV absorbance of DOX and CDs at 488 nm and 330 nm, respectively.
FAC loading ratio was calculated by (mT–mt)/mCDs × 100%, mT is the total FAC in the system and mt is the amount in dialyzate according to the absorbance at 260 nm [16,17].
CDs-drugs (CDs-DOX and CDs-FAC) were characterized by UV and fluorescence spectra, and ζ-potential.

In-vitro release behaviors of CDs-DOX and CDs-FAC were all measured. The release mediums included PBS buffer (pH = 5.0, 6.5 and 7.4), plasma, SGF (artificial gastric juice, contains HCl, NaCl and pepsin, pH = 1.3) and SIF (artificial intestinal juice, includes NaOH, KH2PO4 and trypsin, pH = 7.5).

The pH-dependent release experiment of CDs-DOX was referred the reference [12]: 2 mL CDs-DOX was put into a dialysis bag (MWCO 1000 Da) and placed in 80 mL PBS buffer (pH = 7.4, or 5.0, or 6.5), and then stirred in the dark at 60 rpm/min, 37 °C, in incubator (ZHWY-200B, Zhicheng, China). At regular intervals, 1 mL PBS buffer was taken out and an equal amount of fresh buffer solution was added. The extracted solution is used to calculate the released DOX.
For control, in vitro release of the drugs (DOX and FAC) was also performed.

The SH-SY5Y cells were cultured in DMEM (Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium), containing 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS, Gibco, USA) and 1% penicillin–streptomycin (Gibco, USA). The HepG2 cells were cultured in RPMI (Roswell Park Memorial Institute) 1640 containing 10% FBS and were maintained at 37 °C in 5% CO2 atmosphere.

The effect on cell viability produced by different samples was determined by MTT assay. 1 × 105 cells/mL cells (SH-SY5Y, or HepG2 cells) were seeded in 96-well plates. When the adhesion reaching 70%, the cells were incubated with CDs, DOX or CDs-DOX for 24 h (the concentrations of CDs were 0, 0.625, 1.25, 2.5 and 5 µg/mL, respectively). The cells were then washed by PBS and added 5 mg/mL MTT solution incubating for 4 h, 150 μL DMSO (dimethyl sulfoxide) was added after sucking the solution and absorbance was tested at 490 nm by a multilabel counter (BioTek, USA).
The cell viability of HepG2 cells incubated with CDs-FAC was also measured similarly to the above. The concentrations of FAC were 0, 1, 10, 100, 500 and 1000 µg/mL, respectively.

Four groups’ cells (control, CDs, free drug and CDs-drug) of SH-SY5Y or HepG2 cells were seeded and cellular morphology was photographed using a microscope (Leica, Germany). The cells were stained with DAPI (5 μg/mL) (Sigma, USA) for 5 min, washed thrice, and observed under a fluorescence microscope (Nikon, Tokyo, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence co-localization of CDs and DOX
ce:section.ce:para: The cells were incubated with CDs-DOX for 3 h, and then the fluorescence of CDs (e x = 405 nm) and DOX (e x = 488 nm) was observed respectively by confocal microscopy after washing with PBS buffer.

One-way ANOVA was performed to data using SPSS 16.0 software (IBM, USA). When P was less than 0.05 considered statistically significant. Each experiment was repeated at least 3 times, and the results were presented as mean ± SD.

Fig. 1D depicted the UV–Vis spectrum and fluorescence emission spectra. CDs exhibited a typical absorbance peak at 230–250 nm, which was due to the π → π* transition of C=C [26]. The peak of emission spectra showed red-shifting from 400 nm to 460 nm, and the intensity decreased when the exciting light increased from 300 nm to 400 nm. This phenomenon may indicate that the surface state is close to molecular state, which affects the CDs band gap and increases the complexity of the CDs excited state [27].

FT-IR spectrum showed the surface groups of CDs (Fig. 1E): peak of 3383 cm−1 was the stretching vibration of N–H and O–H, meaning amino and hydroxyl groups [28]. The stretching vibration band of C–H was observed at 2926 cm−1. The peaks at 1595 cm−1 and 1203 cm−1 attributed to the in-plane bending vibration of –NH2 and stretching vibration of C-N, respectively [28]. Fig. 1E suggested that –NH2 and –OH groups, with good water solubility, presented on the surface of CDs. Furthermore, –NH2 group provided the convenience for connecting drugs.

A wide diffraction peak appeared at 2θ = 20°–23°in XRD pattern (Fig. 1F), indicating highly disordered carbon atoms and an amorphous carbon phase [29]. 13C NMR spectroscopy could distinguish sp3-hybridized or sp2-hybridized carbon atoms [30]. In Fig. 1G, peak of 20–40 ppm was related to aliphatic carbon atoms with sp3 hybridization; signals between 60–80 ppm suggested that aliphatic carbon atoms were bound to negatively charged nitrogen and oxygen atoms; and 180 ppm that was confirmed carbon atoms in carboxyl or amide groups with sp2 hybridization. Fig. 1H was the color coordinate of CDs, and the fluorescence position was in the blue region of the color coordinate. The quantum yield (QY) of CDs was 12% using quinine sulphate as reference.

XPS measurement showed that C1s, N1s, and O1s appeared at about 285 eV, 400 eV and 531 eV, respectively (Fig. 2A). The high resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 2B) was disassembled into three peaks attributed to C–C (284.72 eV), C–O (286.05 eV) and C=O (288.21 eV), respectively. N1s spectrum presented C–N (399.76 eV), N–H (401.52 eV) (Fig. 2C). The O1s spectrum (Fig. 2D) was fitted to N–O (531.12 eV) and C–O (532.52 eV). The results also showed that the surface of CDs was rich in –NH2 group.

In UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. 3C), CDs had strong absorption within 230–250 nm, and no absorption between 450–600 nm. DOX had a characteristic absorption peak at 488 nm. After combining with CDs, DOX characteristic peak appeared at 510 nm, indicating the formation of CD-DOX conjugate (Fig. 3C). Fig. 3D depicted the fluorescence emission spectra of CDs, DOX, and CDs-DOX. At the excitation wavelength (λ ex) of 325 nm (to excite CDs), the emission wavelength (λ em) peak of CDs was 400 nm, and the same peak in CDs-DOX red-shifted to 410 nm. At the λ ex of 500 nm (to excite DOX), the two λ em peaks of DOX was at 550 nm-600 nm, and they all blue-shifted in CDs-DOX. In brief, CDs-DOX showed the adduct peaks of CDs and DOX moieties with their own emission characteristics.

CDs also can load FAC to treat iron deficiency disease. CDs-FAC was prepared and characterized by ζ potential, UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. S1). Fig. S1 showed that the optimal mass ratio of CDs and FAC (m/m) was 1:1 and the drug loading was 54.0%. Fig. S1B was the zeta potential of CDs, FAC and CDs-FAC. The zeta potentials of CDs, free FAC and CDs-FAC were −28.3 mV, −4.35 mV, and −3.24 mV, respectively. Obviously, CDs and FAC were incorporated not by electrostatic interaction but by covalent interaction (Scheme S1).

The results of FT-IR and XPS indicated that there were many –NH2 groups on the surface of CDs, which made CDs easily soluble in water and combine with water-soluble drugs. When CDs loading DOX, the –NH2 group (on CDs surface) was reacted with the –C=O group (on DOX surface) to produce –N=C–, that is Schiff’s base, which could be seen at ~1640.21 cm−1 in FTIR spectrum (Fig. S2). The bond of –N=C– was unstable and hydrolyzed easily under acidic conditions. This facilitated the drug targeted release in acidic environments. When CDs loading FAC, the –NH2 group (on CDs) reacted with the –OH group (on FAC) under EDC to produce –CONH– bond, at about 1640.16 cm−1 (Fig. S3). This bond was the ubiquitous peptide bond in organisms and relatively stable in an acidic environment. New covalent bonds were formed in CDs drug loading systems (CDs-DOX and CDs-FAC). We speculated that they had different cytotoxicity and release mechanisms from free drugs.

Iron overload can easily damage nerve cells, so SH-SY5Y (human neuroblastoma cell line SY5Y) was selected as the cell model in our experiment. The cytotoxicity of CDs-DOX was detected in SH-SY5Y cells by MTT method. Cells were incubated at the different DOX concentrations (0.625–5 µg/mL) to examine the toxicity of DOX, CDs–DOX and CDs (Fig. 4 A). The CDs-treated cells showed good cell activity. However, DOX and CDS-DOX significantly inhibited cell growth when the dose was increased from 0.625 mg/mL to 5 mg/mL, and the viability of SH-SY5Y cells decreased to nearly 30% and 41%, respectively. Therefore, DOX had a significantly higher cytotoxicity than the CDs-DOX (p < 0.05). CDs had no cytotoxicity, and the toxicity of CDs-DOX was produced solely by DOX, which maintained its pharmacological activity. Morphology changes of SH-SY5Y cells were also observed (Fig. 4B, C). Fig. 4B showed that the number of living cells in the DOX group was significantly less than that in the control group, and dead cells became round. Being stained by DAPI, the cell nuclei changed to crescent shapes or were lysed (Fig. 4C, DOX group). The number of living cells of CDs–DOX group increased compared with DOX group (Fig. 4B, C). Because that CDs were non-toxic, which did not affect the cell survival and morphology, and DOX maintained its pharmacodynamics activity and had prolonged delivery property.

Iron was mainly stored in the liver, so HepG2 (human hepatica cells) was selected as the experimental model in vitro. MTT assay was also performed using HepG2 cells to determine whether CDs-FAC had cytotoxicity. Then, HepG2 cells were incubated at different FAC concentrations (1–1000 µg/mL) to estimate the toxicity of CDs-FAC, CDs, and FAC (Fig. S4). CDs and CDs-FAC exhibited great cell viability, and CDs-FAC promoted cell proliferation at 500 µg/mL and 1000 µg/mL. Free FAC inhibited cell growth at high doses (100, 500 and 1000 µg/mL), while the same amount of FAC in CDs-FAC maintained the proliferation activity of HepG2 cells. These results illustrated that the carrier (CDs) reduces the toxicity of FAC and CDs-FAC could be a better effect of iron supplementation than FAC.

Confocal was used for co-localization of CDs (e x = 405 nm) and DOX (e x = 488 nm) to observe CDs carried DOX into cells successfully (Fig. 5 ). As can be seen from the Fig. 5, CDs and DOX emit blue and red fluorescence respectively, and overlapped light appears purple.

Currently, chemotherapy was the main treatments for cancer and DOX was one of the most commonly used chemotherapy drugs. The benefits achieved by chemotherapy in advanced cancer were limited for its substantial toxicity effects. So, the great effort has been devoted to study the delivery system and improve the targeted release of DOX to reduce its side effects. It was reported that the pH in the tumor tissue is 6.5–7.2, showing weak acidity, while the lysosomes in the tumor cells are more acidic, which provides an ideal trigger for drug controlled release [31]. Based on this, many researchers have developed a variety of pH-responsive controlled release of drug-loading systems to achieve drug controlled release [9]. In our experiment, the pH-dependent release behaviors of CDs-DOX in vitro were examined in PBS buffer (pH = 5.0, 6.5, 7.4), plasma, SGF and SIF, respectively. The release of CDs-DOX was pH-dependent compared with that of DOX (Fig. 6 A, Fig. S5): at the same time (80 h), DOX release CDs-DOX was 67%, 23%, and 15% at pH = 5.0, 6.5 and 7.4 respectively, which was possibly due to the –C=N– bond of CDs-DOX was unstable and easy to dissociate in an acidic environment. Similarly, CDs-DOX exhibited higher release property in SGF (pH = 1.3) than in PLA (pH = 7.4) and SIF (pH = 7.5), because of the significantly acidic pH of SGF (Fig. 6B). Thus the link bind (–N=C–) between CDs and DOX increased the pH-targeted property of the delivered drugs.

As we all known, the small intestine has a neutral environment and is the main place for nutrient absorption. In general, food is stored in the stomach for 2–3 hours before being absorbed into the small intestine [32]. At the beginning of release periods (2 h), the amount of CDs-FAC released in gastric juice was very small (13%, Fig. 6C), comparing to free FAC (45%, Fig. 6D), indicating that the link bond (–CO–NH–) between CDs and FAC was stable in strong acidity (pH = 1.3) under gastric environment and CDs-FAC could pass through stomach and enter the intestine for absorption.

CDs-FAC exhibited better sustained release property in the presence of enzymes in SIF than in SGF and PLA, and the 100% of FAC release from CDs-FAC took nearly 110 h, which predicted the high absorption efficiency of CDs-FAC (Fig. 6C). By contrast, free FAC had no sustained release property and selectivity in SIF and SGF (Fig. 6D). Therefore, using carbon to transport FAC might improve the oral bioavailability of FAC.

Thermodynamic processes exist in living systems [33,34]. In vitro release of CDs-DOX on the surface of tumor was mimicked in PBS medium (pH = 5.0) (Fig. S6). Four mathematical models were used to fit the release data, including zero-order kinetics, first-order kinetics, Weibull model and Higuchi model. From the fitting data (Table S1), it could be seen that the fitting coefficient (R 2) in Weibull model was the maximum (R2 DOX = 0.96343, R2 CDs-DOX = 0.99582) among the four models, suggesting the drug release of CDs-DOX belonged to Weibull model. Furthermore, the in vitro releases of DOX loaded or not loaded by CDs were same and consistent with Weibull model. The release mechanism of CDs-DOX was also studied by fitting the release data using Ritger-Peppas model. Data suggested that the release mechanism of free DOX was Fick diffusion (n = 0.11554), but when DOX was loaded by CDs, the release mechanism of CDs-DOX was skeleton dissolution (Table S2). This phenomenon was related with the covalent bond (–N=C–) between CDs and DOX. This bond was sensitive to acid, resulting in easy release of DOX from CDs.

Since the absorption of nutrients was in intestine, the in vitro release experiments of CDs-FAC and FAC were stimulated in SIF by the above four mathematical models (Fig. S7). According to the fitting data (Table S3), the release models of CDs-FAC and FAC were both conformed to Weibull models (R2 CDs-FAC = 0.99633 and R2 FAC = 0.97152). The release mechanism of CDs-FAC and FAC were determined by Ritger-Peppas model. According to data, the n values were both less than 0.45 (nCDs-FAC = 0.28659 and nFAC = 0.23633) (Table S4), which was consistent to Fick diffusion.

From the above results, we concluded that the in vitro release models of CDs-DOX and CDs-FAC were all Weibull models, but the release mechanisms were different, due to the different coupling ways between CDs and drugs. The studies of drug release in vitro drug release studies could help us to predict drug use in vivo.

In this work, CDs could load drugs (DOX or FAC) used in the field of iron metabolism. The results showed that CDs could decrease the cytotoxicity and increase the efficacy of free drugs, especially, CDs-DOX had target property and CDs-FAC had high absorption efficacy. The in vitro release models of CDs-drug belonged to Weibull model, and the release mechanism was related to the bonding way between CDs and drug. We speculate that CDs could be used as carriers to transport drugs (containing -C=O group) to improve their pH targeting, and also to carry drugs (containing –COOH group) to improve their intestinal absorbability.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by grants from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31201305) and Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province (B2019205054). We thank Dr. Yingqiang Zhao (Shandong Normal University) for help with the experimental measurements.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jiec.2021.05.046.

ce:section.ce:para:
